attributes.stem:
A 23-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 1-hour history of right wrist pain after falling forwards onto an outstretched hand with his wrist in extension. He is in significant pain with obvious swelling localised to his wrist. On examination, there is tenderness to palpation and limited range of motion due to pain at the right wrist. He also reports some tingling sensation at his thumb, index, and middle fingers that has slowly developed over the past hour.

X-rays are taken (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following types of fractures is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The X-ray shows an intact distal radioulnar joint and obvious fracture with displacement of the radius. This is a Colles fracture, which is an dorsiflexion fracture of the distal radius. A similar fracture is the Barton fracture, where there is extensions of the fracture through the dorsal aspect to the articular surface but not to the volar aspect. The patient's mechanism of fall is the most common presentation for this type of fracture, with associated soft tissue swelling, tenderness to palpation, and limited range of motion at the wrist joint. In Colles fractures, there is usually dorsal displacement of the distal fragment, which can cause complications such as median nerve injury / tension, which is demonstrated in this case as tingling sensation in the thumb, index, and middle fingers. Other rarer complications can include compartment syndrome and radial artery injury.

A Galeazzi fracture is a radial shaft fracture with disruption of the distal radioulnar joint. This is usually caused by a fall on an outstretched arm with pronated forearm. Although there can be similar symptoms to a Colles fracture such as pain, limited range of movement of the wrist joint, and even tingling of the index and middle fingers due to anterior interosseous nerve injury, the X-ray findings of an intact distal radioulnar joint excludes this.

A Smith fracture is a flexion-type fracture, where patients usually report having fallen onto an outstretched arm with the wrist in flexion. This results in volar displacement of the distal fragment, opposite to a Colles fracture. A Smith fracture can also present similarly to this patient, except that the radial nerve is usually affected instead of the median nerve, which can result in impaired sensation of the dorsal lateral half of the hand. The xrays are not consistent with a 'Smith's fracture'

A Monteggia fracture is located at the proximal third of the ulnar shaft, associated with dislocation of the radial head. This type of fracture usually arises from a fall onto an outstretched hand with a pronated forearm. However, patients would complain of elbow joint pain rather than wrist pain, with associated swelling and tenderness to palpation localised to the elbow joint. Furthermore, the x-ray findings involve a fracture of the radius instead of the ulna.

A scaphoid fracture is the second most likely differential in this case, with a similar mechanism of fall to a Colles fracture (extended arm and wrist), along with wrist pain and localised swelling. However, there also would be pain within the anatomical snuffbox and when the scaphoid tubercle is palpated, with decreased grip strength and pain elicited on pinching/grasping, which is not demonstrated in the stem. While a scaphoid fracture can be radiologically occult, this xray clearly demonstrates a fractured distal radius.
learningPoints: Fall onto outstretched hand with wrist extended - Think Colles fracture or scaphoid fracture.
Complications of Colles fracture include median nerve injury, radial artery injury, and compartment syndrome of the forearm.
references: Summers K, Mabrouk A, Fowles SM. Colles' Fracture. InStatPearls [Internet] 2022 Nov 14. StatPearls Publishing.
Fowler JR, Hughes TB. Scaphoid fractures. Clinics in Sports Medicine. 2015 Jan 1;34(1):37-50.
